Text: Quick question, why do stocks suddenly crash, but never warn you before?
Day five of turning you into an interview pattern machine.
Today's weapon, monotonic stack, they give you an array and ask scary questions.
Next, greater element?
Previous smaller? Stock span? Daily temperatures?
Most people panic. They look left? They look right?
Nested loops everywhere. That's slow.
And interviewers hate slow.
Here's the smarter way. Use a stack, but not any stack.
A monotonic stack. That means the stack is always increasing or decreasing.
Now pay attention. You scan the array from left to right.
For each element, while the stack breaks the rule you pop.
When the rule is safe, you push that pop moment. That's the answer.
Why? Because the element being popped just found its next greater or smaller.
One push, one pop. No element is touched twice.
That's why this works. Time complexity becomes O of N.
No backtracking, no rechecking. This pattern shows up everywhere.
Next, greater element? Daily temperatures? Largest rectangle in histogram?
Stock span problem? Once this clicks, array problems start feeling unfairly easy.
Monotonic stack? Control the stack? Reveal the future?
Crack interview.
